family_audit: Close the raw launch-bounds-only occupancy probe as a direct family replay: it regressed to 25.911183 ms even though it successfully cut the hot-band register budget from 200 to 168 and raised occupancy_limit_registers from 2 to 3.
Keep the structural lesson from round 29 alive in a new follow-on family: the occupancy wall moved in the right direction, but barrier stall became the new dominant cost at 10.97%.
Keep the exact PTX restore as a recovery anchor, but continue treating the 24.16-24.18 ms cluster as a plateau rather than a frontier of meaningful new wins.
Defer the non-PTX sibling for this round because it remains a correctness-safe alternate surface with the same plateau signature, not a new headroom source.
Leave the 256x128 low-register transplant family open as a high-risk aggressive branch, but not above the cleaner barrier-amortization continuation.

directions.name: Keep 3-CTA Residency And Amortize Barriers With Two-K Stages
directions.hypothesis: Round 29 proved that the occupancy-first idea was directionally correct even though the end-to-end runtime regressed. The hot-band PTX branch dropped from 200 to 168 registers per thread, moved occupancy_limit_registers from 2 to 3, raised achieved warps from 16.59% to 24.77%, and collapsed long-scoreboard stall from 7.34% to 1.69%. The regression came from a new synchronization wall instead: barrier stall jumped from 5.19% to 10.97% and the hot-band kernel slowed to about 33.76 us. The cleanest next move is therefore not to undo the occupancy gain, but to keep the grouped_rows=4 and 3-CTA residency budget while routing the default hot-band launch onto the existing 128x128x32 two-K-stage kernel so each steady-state barrier protects two K tiles instead of one.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and stage handoff overhead after the residency wall has been partially relaxed.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is still inside the same occupancy-first branch and should preserve correctness semantics more cleanly than reopening 256x128 ownership work, but it can easily give back the register win if the two-K-stage kernel compiles much fatter than expected.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime with a >0.15 ms improvement threshold over the current 25.911183 ms run
ranking_notes: This is the only branch in the repo that currently carries direct evidence of improved occupancy on the 128x128 hot band without correctness loss.
The latest measurement did not falsify the occupancy thesis; it isolated the next cost center as barrier/handoff.
The existing 128x128x32 kernel provides a bounded way to test barrier amortization without inventing a brand-new kernel family from scratch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop this follow-on family if it loses the occupancy gain from round 29 or if barrier stall stays near 10% with no runtime recovery; sub-0.10 ms movements should still be treated as noise.

directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: The exact PTX winner remains the fastest correctness-proven surface in the repo and is still the right recovery anchor after the round-29 regression. If the goal were only to get back to the best known runtime, the correct next move would be to restore 489574e again and discard the failed occupancy probe. It is ranked second only because the user's current guidance explicitly prefers structural headroom moves over another replay of a known plateau anchor.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Known register-limited plateau on the accepted 128x128 PTX surface.
directions.risk: Low. This is the guardrail restore path.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 25.911183 ms run
ranking_notes: Keep this in the frontier as the clean recovery candidate if the round-30 follow-on barrier probe also regresses.
Do not treat a return to 24.16-24.18 ms as a new optimization breakthrough; it is only recovery to the best known plateau.
directions.stop_condition: Use this direction only as a recovery anchor; if the result lands back inside the existing 24.16-24.18 ms band, the next round should still search for a structural move rather than replay the same surface again.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: The round-29 3-CTA probe showed that reducing registers and increasing active warps really does move the machine state, but it also showed how expensive the wrong synchronization pattern can be. The 256x128 family remains the highest-risk, highest-ceiling alternate route because the old half-panel snapshots already demonstrated about 92-93 registers per thread and about 32.9% active warps on this exact shape. The right way to keep that idea alive is still to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot branch and transplant the low-register staging behavior without reviving the broken writer split.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and handoff strategy in the 256x128 hot-band path, plus correctness-sensitive writer ownership.
directions.risk: High. This remains a broad code-motion branch with correctness history against it.
ranking_notes: Keep this family in the live queue because it is still one of the only branches that ever escaped the 16.6%-warps machine state.
Rank it below the barrier-amortized 128x128 continuation because the latter is a much cleaner next experiment on the evidence we just measured.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if correctness breaks again or if the branch stays above about 28-30 ms after recovering the low-register budget.

notes: Round 30 treats round 29 as a structurally useful failure. The launch-bounds probe did exactly what the occupancy diagnosis wanted: registers fell from 200 to 168, occupancy_limit_registers rose from 2 to 3, active warps rose from 16.59% to 24.77%, and long-scoreboard stall collapsed from 7.34% to 1.69%. The runtime regression therefore points to the next missing piece, not a fully wrong premise: barrier stall doubled to 10.97% and the hot-band kernel slowed from about 32.69 us to about 33.76 us. The ranking therefore continues the aggressive path, but narrows it to barrier amortization on top of the newly proven occupancy gain.
